result:
:::writing
The train arrived without announcement, a soft animal sliding into the station’s concrete ribs. People adjusted their bags as if tugged by a shared string, each pretending not to notice the others’ small rituals: the tapping foot, the thumb counting messages, the quick glance at the clock that never quite matches the time inside a person.

A child pressed her face to the window, fog blooming like a secret she couldn’t keep. Across from her, an old man folded a newspaper into smaller and smaller squares, as though he might eventually discover a perfect silence at its center.

When the doors opened, the air shifted—coffee, rain, metal—and for a moment everyone breathed the same story. Then the spell broke. Names returned, destinations hardened, and the current pulled them apart into streets that argued with the sky.

I stayed a beat longer, watching the train empty itself, thinking how departure is just a way of arranging hope so it looks like motion. Outside, a siren stitched urgency through the afternoon, and somewhere a door closed with the confidence of a decision. I stepped forward at last, carrying nothing visible, only the faint warmth of that shared breath, already cooling, already becoming a memory I would later call mine, though it belonged to no one, and therefore to everyone who had been there, waiting, pretending, listening for a signal they could trust enough to move again.

Tomorrow the platform will remember us differently, or not at all.
:::

:::writing
The train arrived without announcement, a soft animal sliding into the station’s concrete ribs. People adjusted their bags as if tugged by a shared string, each pretending not to notice the others’ small rituals: the tapping foot, the thumb counting messages, the quick glance at the clock that never quite matches the time inside a person.

A child pressed her face to the window, fog blooming like a secret she couldn’t keep. Across from her, an old man folded a newspaper into smaller and smaller squares, as though he might eventually discover a perfect silence at its center.

When the doors opened, the air shifted—coffee, rain, metal—and for a moment everyone breathed the same story. Then the spell broke. Names returned, destinations hardened, and the current pulled them apart into streets that argued with the sky.

I stayed a beat longer, watching the train empty itself, thinking how departure is just a way of arranging hope so it looks like motion. Outside, a siren stitched urgency through the afternoon, and somewhere a door closed with the confidence of a decision. I stepped forward at last, carrying nothing visible, only the faint warmth of that shared breath, already cooling, already becoming a memory I would later call mine, though it belonged to no one, and therefore to everyone who had been there, waiting, pretending, listening for a signal they could trust enough to move again.

Tomorrow the platform will remember us differently, or not at all.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
